items.question_text: Use TBL 1-1-1. A proposed change must be fully coordinated and signed by August 7, 2025. Which publication does that cutoff support?
items.explanation: TBL 1-1-1 maps the August 7, 2025 cutoff to Change 2, effective January 22, 2026.

choices.text: Change 1, effective August 7, 2025.
Change 2, effective January 22, 2026.
Change 3, effective July 9, 2026.
JO 7110.65CC, effective December 24, 2026.

items.question_text: Use TBL 1-1-3. A waiver involves Army military operations or facilities. Which headquarters interface office is listed?
items.explanation: TBL 1-1-3 lists the Army interface as Director, USAASA (MOAS-AS), Ft. Belvoir, Virginia.

choices.text: Chief of Naval Operations, N980A, NAATSEA.
HQ AFFSA, Oklahoma City.
Director, USAASA (MOAS-AS), Ft. Belvoir.
FAA Flight Standards District Office.

items.question_text: Use FIG 1-2-1. In this order, what does the figure help interpret when the text says tracks or courses diverge?
items.explanation: FIG 1-2-1 is the visual reference for divergence, supporting later rules that depend on whether courses, tracks, or paths separate by a required angle.

choices.text: Whether two courses or tracks are separating from each other by an angle.
Whether an aircraft is approved for due regard operations.
Whether time should be rounded to the nearest quarter minute.
Whether miles are nautical or statute miles.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 2-4-17 #1, TABLE 2-4-17 #2, and TABLE 2-4-17 #3. For serial numbers, what is the controlling number-pronunciation principle?
items.explanation: TABLE 2-4-17 #1, TABLE 2-4-17 #2, and TABLE 2-4-17 #3 show serial-number examples. Serial numbers are spoken as separate digits.

choices.text: Speak the separate digits.
Use group form whenever possible.
Read only the last three digits.
Convert the number to local time.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 2-4-17 #4 and TABLE 2-4-17 #5. Which pairing correctly distinguishes altitude/flight-level wording from MDA/DH wording?
items.explanation: TABLE 2-4-17 #4 covers flight levels and TABLE 2-4-17 #5 covers MDA/DH altitude examples. Flight levels use 'flight level' plus separate digits; MDA/DH uses the altitude type followed by separate digits.

choices.text: Flight levels use 'flight level' plus separate digits; MDA/DH states the altitude type and separate digits.
Flight levels are always spoken in group form; MDA/DH values are rounded to the nearest thousand.
Flight levels omit the words 'flight level'; MDA/DH values are given only in local time.
Both are spoken as runway designations.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 2-4-17 #6, TABLE 2-4-17 #7, TABLE 2-4-17 #8, and TABLE 2-4-17 #9. What is the difference between general UTC time, requested local time, time checks, and abbreviated time?
items.explanation: TABLE 2-4-17 #6 and TABLE 2-4-17 #7 show UTC/local-time statements; TABLE 2-4-17 #8 shows time checks with nearest quarter-minute treatment; TABLE 2-4-17 #9 shows abbreviated minute-only time.

choices.text: They use different authorized forms depending on whether full UTC, requested local equivalent, time-check precision, or minute-only abbreviation is being stated.
They all require the word 'local' and never use UTC.
They all omit the hour and state only minutes.
They all require group form to avoid separate digits.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 2-4-17 #10, TABLE 2-4-17 #11, TABLE 2-4-17 #12, and TABLE 2-4-17 #13. Which item correctly pairs the required lead word with the number type?
items.explanation: TABLE 2-4-17 #10 through TABLE 2-4-17 #13 cover field elevation, use of zero/group form, altimeter setting, and heading examples.

choices.text: 'Field elevation' for elevation, 'altimeter' for setting, 'heading' for assigned heading.
'Runway' for every number that contains a zero.
'Mach' for field elevations and altimeter settings.
No lead word is used for heading or altimeter settings.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 2-4-17 #14, TABLE 2-4-17 #15, TABLE 2-4-17 #16, TABLE 2-4-17 #17, TABLE 2-4-17 #18, and TABLE 2-4-17 #19. Which statement captures the distinction among codes, runways, frequencies, speed, and Mach?
items.explanation: TABLE 2-4-17 #14 through TABLE 2-4-17 #19 show that codes, runway designations, frequencies, speeds, and Mach numbers each have prescribed phraseology rather than one universal number-reading pattern.

choices.text: Each type has its own prescribed phraseology; do not apply one generic number-reading rule to all of them.
All are read as four separate UTC digits.
All are read only in group form.
Only Mach numbers require a lead word; the others omit context.

items.question_text: Use TBL 3-4-8. At night, visibility is 4 miles and no pilot request or local directive changes the setting. What HIRL/RCLS/TDZL step applies?
items.explanation: TBL 3-4-8 assigns step 2 at night for visibility from 3 to 5 miles inclusive.

choices.text: Step 1.
Step 2.
Step 3.
Step 5.

items.question_text: Use TBL 3-4-9. HIRL controls the associated MALSR. At night with visibility greater than 5 miles, which step is shown absent a pilot request or controller judgment override?
items.explanation: TBL 3-4-9 assigns step 1 at night when visibility is more than 5 miles.

choices.text: Step 1.
Step 2.
Step 4.
Step 5.

items.question_text: Use TBL 3-4-10. A MIRL system is being operated during the day with visibility 2.5 miles. Which intensity step applies unless a pilot request or controller judgment controls?
items.explanation: TBL 3-4-10 assigns MIRL step 2 during the day for visibility from 2 to 3 miles.

choices.text: Step 1.
Step 2.
Step 3.
Step 5.

items.question_text: Use TBL 3-4-11, TBL 3-4-12, and TBL 3-4-13. What is the first decision before selecting a taxiway light setting?
items.explanation: TBL 3-4-11, TBL 3-4-12, and TBL 3-4-13 are separated by taxiway light control type: three-step, five-step, and one-step systems.

choices.text: Determine whether the taxiway lights are three-step, five-step, or one-step.
Always use step 5 for taxiway lights.
Use runway centerline light settings for all taxiway lights.
Turn taxiway lights off when visibility is less than 1 mile.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-1 and TABLE 3-9-6 #1. Same-runway departure separation depends on what operational relationship?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-1 depicts same-runway departure spacing, and TABLE 3-9-6 #1 flags the wake-turbulence application. The controller must account for runway relationship, airborne path, and wake category where applicable.

choices.text: Whether the following departure will use the same runway/path and whether wake turbulence applies.
Only whether both aircraft have the same destination.
Only whether the first aircraft is IFR.
Only whether both aircraft use the same beacon code.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 3-9-8 #1. For intersecting-runway departure wake application, what must be protected when the second departure crosses the airborne path of the first?
items.explanation: TABLE 3-9-8 #1 marks the wake-turbulence application for intersecting-runway departures where the second aircraft crosses the airborne path of the first.

choices.text: The appropriate wake-turbulence interval or radar separation.
Only runway occupancy time after landing.
Only the aircraft identification format.
Only a published speed restriction.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 3-9-9 #1. Adjacent-airport departure wake application is triggered by which geometry?
items.explanation: TABLE 3-9-9 #1 flags adjacent-airport departure wake application when the departure will cross the flight path of a preceding departure from another airport.

choices.text: The departure crosses the flight path of a preceding aircraft from an adjacent airport.
The two aircraft file the same cruise altitude.
Both aircraft use the same terminal frequency.
The second aircraft is assigned a visual approach.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-1 and TABLE 3-10-3 #1. In same-runway arrival separation, why does the controller need both the runway-occupancy picture and the wake table context?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-1 depicts same-runway arrival spacing, while TABLE 3-10-3 #1 identifies wake-turbulence application. The aircraft must be separated for runway use and, where applicable, wake turbulence.

choices.text: Because runway-use separation and wake-turbulence separation may both control the arrival sequence.
Because wake turbulence replaces all runway separation rules.
Because the figure is only for aircraft identification.
Because same-runway arrivals never require wake consideration.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-6, FIG 3-10-7, FIG 3-10-8, and FIG 3-10-9. For intersecting-runway arrivals, what status of the preceding arrival supports issuing the succeeding landing clearance?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-6 through FIG 3-10-9 illustrate preceding-arrival cases. The preceding arrival must be clear of the landing runway, complete landing roll and hold short, or have passed the intersection/flight path as applicable.

choices.text: The preceding arrival is clear, will hold short after landing roll, or has passed the intersection/flight path.
The preceding arrival has merely crossed the threshold.
The succeeding aircraft has the same destination airport.
Both aircraft are using the same callsign prefix.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-10, FIG 3-10-11, FIG 3-10-12, FIG 3-10-13, and TABLE 3-10-4 #1. In LAHSO/intersecting flight path operations, what remains true even when land-and-hold-short instructions are issued?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-10 through FIG 3-10-13 show LAHSO/intersecting-runway situations, and TABLE 3-10-4 #1 flags wake application. LAHSO does not relieve controllers from applying other required separation, including wake when applicable.

choices.text: Other required separation, including wake turbulence where applicable, still has to be provided.
LAHSO cancels all wake-turbulence requirements.
Traffic information is optional once landing clearance is issued.
The hold-short point must always be the runway intersection itself.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-14. What does the overhead maneuver figure help a controller visualize?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-14 depicts the overhead maneuver pattern so the controller can understand the aircraft's break, downwind, and landing sequence relative to other airport traffic.

choices.text: The pattern geometry and sequence used in an overhead maneuver.
The CPDLC message element for route uplinks.
The publication cutoff date for a change.
The shape of an unmanned free balloon.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-15, FIG 3-10-16, and FIG 3-10-17. What is the shared training value of the simulated flameout and emergency landing pattern figures?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-15, FIG 3-10-16, and FIG 3-10-17 show the flight-path geometry for simulated flameout or emergency landing patterns so the controller can anticipate where the aircraft will be in relation to the runway and other traffic.

choices.text: They show flight-path geometry that affects sequencing, traffic advisories, and runway-use awareness.
They prescribe CPDLC response attributes.
They define aircraft type designators.
They replace all local emergency procedures.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 3-11-1 #1. For helicopter/powered-lift operations, why is the wake turbulence application table context still relevant?
items.explanation: TABLE 3-11-1 #1 marks wake-turbulence application in the helicopter/powered-lift section; the controller must not treat helicopter geometry as eliminating wake considerations.

choices.text: Because wake turbulence can still control spacing or sequencing in the depicted helicopter/powered-lift operation.
Because helicopters are exempt from all wake rules.
Because the table is only a publication schedule.
Because it defines CPDLC route messages.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-11-1 and FIG 3-11-2. What condition must exist before a helicopter or powered-lift departure is separated from a preceding helicopter/powered-lift operation using these figures?
items.explanation: FIG 3-11-1 and FIG 3-11-2 depict helicopter departure separation cases, including the preceding operation being clear of the departure surface or having taxied off the landing area.

choices.text: The preceding operation is clear of the relevant departure/landing area as depicted.
The departure has the same requested altitude.
The aircraft are on the same CPDLC connection.
The aircraft share the same equipment suffix.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-11-3, FIG 3-11-4, and FIG 3-11-5. For helicopter/powered-lift arrival separation, what does the controller use the figures to judge?
items.explanation: FIG 3-11-3, FIG 3-11-4, and FIG 3-11-5 depict helicopter arrival separation geometries and help determine whether the preceding traffic is clear enough for the arriving operation.

choices.text: Whether the preceding traffic and landing/departure area relationship permits the arriving operation.
Whether the aircraft is RVSM capable.
Whether the runway lights should be step 5.
Whether the aircraft identification has seven characters.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-12-5. What airport-surface relationship is the sea lane arrival operations figure meant to clarify?
items.explanation: FIG 3-12-5 depicts sea lane arrival operations so controllers can visualize the relationship of the water operating area to other airport movement or runway operations.

choices.text: How sea lane arrival paths relate to other airport surface or runway operations.
How to pronounce Mach numbers.
How to select ADS-C reporting intervals.
How to determine publication effective dates.

items.question_text: Use FIG 4-1-1 and FIG 4-1-2. When assigning a route other than an established airway or route, what do the altitude-and-distance limitation figures help verify?
items.explanation: FIG 4-1-1 and FIG 4-1-2 illustrate how altitude and distance limitations apply when a route is not on an established airway/route.

choices.text: That the non-airway route segment stays within the applicable altitude and distance limitations.
That all aircraft use the same route clearance message element.
That the aircraft has reported a balloon altitude.
That runway occupancy time is less than 50 seconds.

items.question_text: Use FIG 4-5-1 and FIG 4-5-2. What is the key distinction between the two minimum crossing altitude cases?
items.explanation: FIG 4-5-1 shows the case with no MCA specified, while FIG 4-5-2 shows the case where an MCA is specified.

choices.text: Whether an MCA is specified for the route segment or fix.
Whether the aircraft is using CPDLC.
Whether a helicopter is departing from a landing area.
Whether an ADS-C report is overdue.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 4-8-1 #1. In RNAV route assignment, what does the RNAV application table context remind the controller to verify?
items.explanation: TABLE 4-8-1 #1 flags RNAV application. The controller must ensure the clearance and aircraft capability/procedure context support RNAV use rather than treating RNAV as a generic route phrase.

choices.text: That the RNAV clearance is appropriate to the aircraft capability and applicable procedure context.
That the aircraft is always cleared direct regardless of capability.
That only voice communication may be used.
That runway separation is not required.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 5-5-4 #1 and TABLE 5-5-4 #2. When applying reduced radar separation minima, why do the wake turbulence application tables matter?
items.explanation: TABLE 5-5-4 #1 and TABLE 5-5-4 #2 flag wake-turbulence application. Reduced radar minima do not erase wake turbulence requirements when wake categories and geometry require them.

choices.text: Wake turbulence requirements may still control even when radar separation minima are reduced.
Reduced radar minima always cancel wake turbulence.
Wake turbulence applies only to CPDLC aircraft.
Wake turbulence is only an aircraft-identification issue.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 6-1-4 #1. In adjacent-airport operation, what is the wake turbulence decision centered on?
items.explanation: TABLE 6-1-4 #1 flags wake application for adjacent-airport operations. The key decision is whether the aircraft will cross the flight path of an aircraft requiring wake separation and what interval applies.

choices.text: Whether the aircraft crosses the flight path of traffic requiring wake separation and which interval applies.
Whether the aircraft uses CPDLC instead of voice.
Whether the runway has centerline lights.
Whether the aircraft is in a holding pattern.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 6-1-5 #1. For arrival minima, what does the wake turbulence application table add to the runway relationship?
items.explanation: TABLE 6-1-5 #1 flags arrival wake application. The controller must combine runway relationship with leading/following aircraft wake categories and the applicable interval or radar distance.

choices.text: Leading/following wake category and the applicable time or distance interval.
Only the destination airport identifier.
Only the aircraft equipment suffix.
Only the published CPDLC message response attribute.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-11, FIG 6-4-12, FIG 6-4-13, FIG 6-4-14, FIG 6-4-15, and FIG 6-4-16. When the earlier speed-differential or climb/descent conditions cannot be met, what longitudinal minimum family do these figures support?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-11 through FIG 6-4-16 support the fallback same/crossing course and altitude-through separation diagrams used when the preceding subparagraph conditions cannot be met.

choices.text: A fallback longitudinal separation family for same/crossing courses and altitude-through cases.
A CPDLC route-uplink family.
A runway light intensity family.
An aircraft-identity prefix family.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-17 and FIG 6-4-18. One aircraft uses DME/ATD while the other uses conventional fix/time reporting. What same-reference condition is required for the 30-mile rule?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-17 and FIG 6-4-18 support the 30-mile case when one aircraft uses DME/ATD and the other does not. The DME/ATD distance must reference the same NAVAID or waypoint over which the other aircraft reported, and the non-DME/ATD aircraft must be within 15 minutes of it.

choices.text: The DME/ATD aircraft references the same NAVAID/waypoint over which the other aircraft reported, with the non-DME/ATD aircraft within 15 minutes.
The aircraft merely have the same destination airport.
Both aircraft must be using ADS-C periodic reports.
The following aircraft must be Mach 0.06 faster.

items.question_text: Use TABLE 6-7-5 #1. For successive approaches, when can wake turbulence increase the basic 2-minute or 5-mile interval?
items.explanation: TABLE 6-7-5 #1 flags wake application for interval minima. Wake categories can require larger time or radar intervals than the basic successive-approach minimum.

choices.text: When the leading/following wake categories and operation type require a larger time or radar interval.
Only when the aircraft are using CPDLC.
Never; the basic 2-minute interval is absolute.
Only when the aircraft are below FL180.

items.question_text: Use FIG 9-6-1. Why should controllers avoid assuming an unmanned free balloon has a constant aircraft-like shape at all altitudes?
items.explanation: FIG 9-6-1 shows shapes of an 11 million cubic feet balloon at various altitudes. The paragraph notes balloon shape changes with altitude and accurate altitude information is needed before using vertical separation.

choices.text: The balloon's physical shape changes at different altitudes, affecting how traffic advisories and separation judgment are framed.
The figure defines runway centerline spacing.
The figure is a CPDLC message table.
The balloon always descends at aircraft cruise speed.
